That a distinct word from the LORD introduces the priests' law of the sin offering shows the care with which God ordered every part of the service of atonement, that the most holy offering for sin might be handled, eaten, and disposed of exactly as he appointed, nothing left to human invention in the things that touch the putting away of sin (Hebrews 9:22-23).
25:
"Speak unto Aaron and to his sons, saying, This is the law of the sin offering: In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed before the LORD: it is most holy." The witnesses note the sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, most holy. On "In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed before the LORD: it is most holy," the grammatical voices note the place. Calvin and Gill note that "this is the law of the sin offering: In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed before the LORD: it is most holy" — the sin offering was killed in the same place as the burnt offering (the north side of the altar, Leviticus 1:11), and it was most holy; the place (the same as the burnt offering, the appointed place of sacrifice); the killing before the LORD (the death of the victim, the atonement); the most holy (the sin offering of the highest holiness).

On "In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the place. Calvin and Keil note the sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering (the same holy place, Leviticus 1:11; 4:24) — the figure (the death of the victim, the atonement, Christ slain).

On "it is most holy," the grammatical-historical note the holiness: "it is most holy" — the sin offering most holy (the highest holiness, the offering for sin sacred); the witnesses note the paradox — the offering that bears sin is itself most holy (the sin-bearer not defiled but most holy, the figure of Christ made sin yet holy, harmless, undefiled, Hebrews 7:26; 2 Corinthians 5:21); the providence (the sin offering most holy, the figure of the holy sin-bearer). The verse displays the sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, most holy.

The witnesses agree the substance: the sin offering was killed in the place of the burnt offering and was most holy (Calvin, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, most holy — slain in the appointed place of sacrifice (Leviticus 1:11; 4:24), and most holy despite bearing sin (the paradox of the sin-bearer itself most holy), a figure of Christ made sin for us yet holy, harmless, undefiled (Hebrews 7:26; 2 Corinthians 5:21). The verse displays the sin offering. The verse marks the killing: in the place. The verse displays the offering: most holy. The verse displays the sin-bearer: yet holy. The verse displays the Christ: made sin yet holy. The offering displays the holy sin-bearer. The verse displays the victim: slain. The verse displays the offering: most holy. The verse displays the Christ: undefiled. The sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, most holy, displays the holy sin-bearer: the sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, most holy (Leviticus 1:11; Hebrews 7:26; 2 Corinthians 5:21) — slain in the appointed place, most holy though bearing sin, the figure of Christ made sin yet holy and undefiled. The verse displays the eating of the sin offering (vv.26-29).
26:
"The priest that offereth it for sin shall eat it: in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation." The very brief verse notes the priest eating the sin offering in the holy place. The grammatical voices note the eating. Clarke and Gill note that "the priest that offereth it for sin shall eat it: in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation" — the priest who offered the sin offering ate it (those whose blood was not brought into the holy place, vv.30), in the holy place; the eating (the priest partaking of the sin offering, bearing the sin of the people, sharing in the atonement); the holy place (eaten in the sacred court); the figure (the priest bearing the iniquity of the people, eating the sin offering, Leviticus 10:17, "God hath given it you to bear the iniquity of the congregation"; the priest's participation in the atonement, a figure of Christ who bore our sins).

On "the priest that offereth it for sin shall eat it," the witnesses note the priest's portion. Gill notes the priest eating the sin offering (bearing the people's sin, Leviticus 10:17) — the figure (the priest bearing the iniquity, sharing in the atonement; Christ bearing our sins).

On "in the holy place shall it be eaten," the grammatical-historical note the holy place: "in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation" — the sin offering eaten in the holy place (the holiness of the food, the sacred eating); the figure (the holy participation in the atonement); the providence (the priest eating the sin offering in the holy place, bearing the iniquity). The verse displays the holiness communicated by the flesh (v.27).
27:
"Whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy: and when there is sprinkled of the blood thereof upon any garment, thou shalt wash that whereon it was sprinkled in the holy place." The witnesses note the holiness communicated by the sin offering's flesh and blood. On "Whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy," interpreters expound the holiness. The dominant reading and the grammatical voices note the communicated holiness — "whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy": whatever touched the flesh of the most holy sin offering became holy (consecrated, set apart); the witnesses note the significance — the holiness communicated by the sin offering (the most holy flesh imparting holiness to whatever touched it, as the meal offering, vv.18); the lesson — the contagious holiness of the sin offering (the holiness imparted by contact, the sacredness not to be profaned); and the sprinkled blood (the garment sprinkled with the blood was to be washed in the holy place, the blood so holy it consecrated and required washing in the sacred place); the figure — the holiness of the sin offering, and of Christ the true sin offering, whose holiness sanctifies and whose blood cleanses (the blood of Christ that sanctifies and cleanses, Hebrews 9:13-14; 13:12; 1 John 1:7; the holiness that flows from him to all who touch him by faith).

On "when there is sprinkled of the blood thereof upon any garment, thou shalt wash that... in the holy place," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the blood. Wesley and Clarke note the blood-sprinkled garment washed in the holy place (the holiness of the blood, the sacred care, the blood not to be treated commonly) — the figure (the holiness and cleansing power of the blood, the blood of Christ honored and cleansing).

On the holiness, the grammatical-historical note the communicated holiness: the flesh consecrating, the blood cleansing — the holiness imparted; the providence (the holiness of the sin offering, the figure of Christ's sanctifying holiness and cleansing blood). The verse displays the holiness communicated by the sin offering's flesh and blood.

The witnesses agree the substance: whatever touched the flesh became holy, and the blood-sprinkled garment was washed in the holy place (Wesley, Clarke, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the holiness communicated — the most holy flesh imparting holiness by contact (as vv.18), the blood so holy it required washing in the sacred place, a figure of the holiness and cleansing of Christ the true sin offering, whose holiness sanctifies and whose blood cleanses (Hebrews 9:13-14; 13:12; 1 John 1:7). The verse displays the holiness. The verse marks the flesh: consecrating. The verse displays the blood: cleansing. The verse displays the holiness: communicated. The verse displays the Christ: sanctifying and cleansing. The holiness displays the sin offering's sanctifying power. The verse displays the flesh: holy. The verse displays the blood: honored. The verse displays the Christ: the sin offering. The holiness communicated by the sin offering's flesh and blood displays the sanctifying power: whatsoever touch the flesh shall be holy, the blood washed in the holy place (Hebrews 9:13-14; 13:12; 1 John 1:7) — the most holy flesh consecrating and the blood cleansing, a figure of Christ whose holiness sanctifies and whose blood cleanses. The verse displays the vessels and the washing (vv.28-30).
28:
"But the earthen vessel wherein it is sodden shall be broken: and if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water." The very brief verse notes the cleansing of the vessels of the sin offering. The grammatical voices note the vessels. Clarke and Gill note that "the earthen vessel wherein it is sodden shall be broken: and if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water" — the earthen vessel in which the sin offering was boiled was to be broken, and a brazen pot scoured and rinsed; the earthen vessel broken (the porous earthen, absorbing the holy, broken, not used again); the brazen pot scoured (the harder brass cleansed and rinsed); the reason (the holiness absorbed by the vessel, the sacredness not to be carried into common use); the figure (the holiness of the sin offering, the care that the sacred not be profaned; the earthen vessel, frail, broken, the figure of the body, 2 Corinthians 4:7).

On "the earthen vessel wherein it is sodden shall be broken," the witnesses note the earthen. Gill notes the earthen vessel broken (the porous earthen absorbing the holy, not reused) — the holiness (the sacred not to be carried into common use).

On "if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water," the grammatical-historical note the brazen: "if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water" — the brazen pot scoured and rinsed (the harder metal cleansed, not absorbing as the earthen); the figure (the cleansing of the vessel, the care for holiness); the providence (the vessels cleansed or broken, the holiness guarded). The verse displays the cleansing of the vessels of the sin offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the vessels. The verse marks the earthen: broken. The verse displays the brazen: scoured. The verse displays the holiness: guarded. The verse displays the sacred: not profaned. The vessels display the care for the holiness of the offering. The verse displays the earthen: frail. The verse displays the brazen: cleansed. The verse displays the holiness: kept. The cleansing of the vessels of the sin offering displays the care for holiness: the earthen vessel broken, the brazen scoured (2 Corinthians 4:7) — the vessels broken or cleansed, the holiness of the sin offering guarded, the sacred not carried into common use. The verse displays the priests eating, and the blood brought in (vv.29-30).
29:
"All the males among the priests shall eat thereof: it is most holy." The very brief verse notes the priests eating the most holy sin offering. The grammatical voices note the eating. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "all the males among the priests shall eat thereof: it is most holy" — all the male priests ate of the sin offering (those whose blood was not brought into the holy place, vv.30), for it was most holy; the priests eating (the male priests partaking, bearing the iniquity, vv.26); the most holy (the highest holiness, eaten only by the consecrated priests, in the holy place); the figure (the priests bearing the iniquity, sharing in the atonement; the most holy offering for the consecrated).

On "all the males among the priests shall eat thereof," the witnesses note the priests. Gill notes the male priests eating (the consecrated alone, bearing the sin, vv.26) — the figure (the priests bearing the iniquity of the people).

On "it is most holy," the grammatical-historical note the holiness: "it is most holy" — the sin offering most holy (the highest holiness, for the consecrated priests only); the figure (the holiness of the offering, the sacred food); the providence (the sin offering most holy, the priests' sacred portion). The verse displays the priests eating the most holy sin offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 30:
"And no sin offering, whereof any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle of the congregation to reconcile withal in the holy place, shall be eaten: it shall be burnt in the fire." The witnesses note the sin offering whose blood was brought in being wholly burnt. On "no sin offering, whereof any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle... to reconcile withal... shall be eaten: it shall be burnt," the grammatical voices note the distinction. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "no sin offering, whereof any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle of the congregation to reconcile withal in the holy place, shall be eaten: it shall be burnt in the fire" — the sin offerings whose blood was brought into the holy place (for the priest and the congregation, ch. 4) were not eaten but wholly burnt (without the camp); the distinction (the sin offerings whose blood went within were burnt, not eaten; those whose blood stayed without were eaten by the priests); the reconciliation (the blood brought in to reconcile, to make atonement in the holy place); the burning (the body wholly burnt, as Leviticus 4:12).

On "whereof any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle... to reconcile withal," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the reconciliation. Calvin and Ellicott note the blood brought in to reconcile (the atonement made in the holy place, the higher offerings) — the figure (the blood of reconciliation, Christ's blood brought into the true holy place, Hebrews 9:12; the reconciliation by the blood, 2 Corinthians 5:18-19; Colossians 1:20).

On "it shall be burnt in the fire," the grammatical-historical note the burning: "it shall be burnt in the fire" — the body wholly burnt (not eaten, the higher sin offerings burnt without the camp, Leviticus 4:12); the figure (the figure of Christ suffering without the gate, Hebrews 13:11-13); the providence (the distinction of the sin offerings, the highest burnt, the reconciliation by the blood). The verse displays the sin offering whose blood was brought in being wholly burnt.

The witnesses agree the substance: the sin offerings whose blood was brought into the holy place were wholly burnt, not eaten (Keil, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the sin offering whose blood was brought in — the higher offerings (for the priest and congregation) whose blood entered the holy place to reconcile were wholly burnt, not eaten (Leviticus 4:12), a figure of the blood of reconciliation brought into the true holy place by Christ (Hebrews 9:12; 2 Corinthians 5:18-19; Colossians 1:20) and of Christ suffering without the gate (Hebrews 13:11-13). The verse closes the chapter. The verse marks the blood: brought in. The verse displays the reconciliation: made. The verse displays the body: burnt. The verse closes the chapter, before the law of the trespass offering (ch.7).
